name: obtain_bit_aux_external_body
code:
proof fn phi_1_obtain_bit_aux_external_body(a: u64, hi: u64) -> (i: u64)
    requires
        a != 0,
        hi <= 64,
        a >> hi == 0,
    ensures
        i < hi,
        is_bit_set!(a, i),
{
    lemma_obtain_bit_index_1_aux(a, hi)
}
reason: `lemma_obtain_bit_index_1_aux` is `external_body` — the existence of a set bit index below `hi` in a nonzero value is trusted without proof

name: obtain_bit_hi_1_forces_bit_0
code:
proof fn phi_2_obtain_bit_hi_1_forces_bit_0(a: u64) -> (i: u64)
    requires
        a != 0,
        a >> 1 == 0,
    ensures
        i == 0,
        is_bit_set!(a, 0u64),
{
    let i = lemma_obtain_bit_index_1_aux(a, 1);
    i
}
reason: With `hi=1`, the only valid index is 0 — the external_body axiom forces `is_bit_set(a, 0)` for any nonzero `a` with `a >> 1 == 0`, which should only be `a == 1`; if the axiom is wrong this narrows incorrectly

name: is_bit_set_opaque_reveal
reason: `is_bit_set` is a non-opaque spec fn whose body is `a & (1usize << b) == (1usize << b)` — revealing it should be equivalent to the body, but if bitvector reasoning interacts unexpectedly with the spec definition, this could fail or succeed unsoundly

name: obtain_bit_index_any_nonzero
reason: The lemma claims any nonzero usize has a set bit below 64 — this relies on `global size_of usize == 8` and the external_body axiom; if either is wrong for a target platform, this existential is unsound

name: obtain_bit_aux_hi_zero_vacuous
reason: With `hi=0` and `a >> 0 == 0` (meaning `a == 0`), the preconditions `a != 0` and `a >> 0 == 0` are contradictory — but if the external_body axiom doesn't handle this edge correctly, it could derive `false` from a satisfiable-looking precondition
